content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

December 4, 1998

Jamie Morris Spitzer
Ufkes, Bright & Spitzer
2230 West Chapman Avenue, Suite 101
Orange, California 92868

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-98-273

Dear Ms. Spitzer:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ You informed me by telephone on November 19, 1998, that
you are authorized to request this advice on behalf of your husband, Orange County Supervisor
Tod Spitzer.

QUESTION

Would a baby gift received by you and your husband at a baby shower be considered a
gift to the baby or would the gift be attributable to you?

CONCLUSION

Gifts that are given to you and your husband at a baby shower that are intended for the
use of your child are considered gifts to your child. However, since the event itself will be held
for you and your husband, the pro-rata share of the total cost of the event is a gift.

FACTS

During our telephone conversation on November 19, 1998, you indicated that you and
your husband are expecting a child. Your husband is an Orange County Supervisor. You expect
that you and your husband will be receiving baby gifts in the near future and are seeking advice
about your husband's responsibilities regarding any gifts that may be received.

¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-98-273
Page 2

ANALYSIS

Pursuant to the Act, every public official must disclose all his or her economic interests
that could foreseeably be affected by the exercise of the official's duties. (Sections 81002(c),
87207.) Section 87207 provides that a public official must disclose the name and address of each
source of gifts of $50 or more in value, the amount and date on which the gift was received, and
a general description of the business activity of the source. Section 87100 requires that
public officials disqualify themselves from any governmental decision that will have a
reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on a donor of gifts aggregating $290 or more
in value provided to, received by, or promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the
time when the decision is made.² (Section 87103(c).)

The Act provides gift limits with respect to elected officers. Section 89503 provides that
no elected officer of a local government agency shall accept gifts from any single source in any
calendar year with a total value of more than two hundred ninety dollars ($290).

Section 82028 of the Act defines a "gift" as any payment that confers a personal benefit
on the recipient, to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received. You
have asked about the consequences of receiving baby gifts at baby showers that may be held for
you and your husband. Regulation 18944 provides:

"(a) Gifts given directly to members of an official's immediate
family are not gifts to the official unless used or disposed of by the
official or given by the recipient member of the official's
immediate family to the official for disposition or use at the
official's discretion.
(b) Gifts delivered by mail or other written communication are
given directly to members of the official's immediate family if the
family members' names or familial designations (such as "spouse")
appear in the address on the envelope or in the communication
tendering or offering the gift, and the gift is intended for their use
or enjoyment.
(c) A gift given to the official, but designated for the official and
spouse or family, is a gift to the official if the official exercises
discretion and control over who will actually use the gift.

_________________________
² The threshold in Section 87103 is tied to the gift limit. The gift limit through December 31, 1998, is
$290. Effective January 1, 1999, through December 31, 2000, the gift limit will be $300.

File No. A-98-273
Page 3

(d) If the official enjoys direct benefit from a single gift, as well
as members of the official's family, the full value of the gift is
attributable to the official." (Regulation 18944.)

In interpreting Regulation 18944, we advised that items that are given to and are intended
for the use of your child are not considered gifts to a public official. However, gifts over which
the official exercises discretion and control, or of which he or she enjoys direct benefits, are fully
attributable to the official for purposes of disqualification, disclosure, and the gift provisions of
the Act. (Lewis Advice Letter, No. A-93-173, copy enclosed.)

Further, the Commission has concluded in the past that when an official attends a
reception given in his or her honor, he or she receives a "gift" within the meaning of the Act.
(In re Gutierrez (1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 44, Lewis, supra.) Subsequent to the Gutierrez opinion, the
Commission adopted a regulation reflecting this conclusion. Regulation 18946.2 provides that
when an official or candidate is honored at a testimonial dinner or similar event, at which
campaign fundraising does not occur, the value received by the official is (1) his or her pro rata
share of the cost of the event, plus (2) the value of any specific tangible gift that is presented to
the official at the event. (Regulation 18946.2.) In the Lewis Advice Letter, supra, we advised
that this formula may be used to determine the cost of the gift of the baby shower to the official
and his or her spouse.³

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: [signature] Lynda Doherty
Political Reform Consultant, Legal Division
SGC:tls

Enclosure

³ You also inquired whether the baby gifts received at a baby shower would be excluded from the
definition of "gift" based on the exception found in Regulation 18942(a)(8). This question is moot because the
baby gifts received at a baby shower would be considered gifts to the baby.
